During the Revolutionary War, General Washington was riding past a detachment of his men, and he saw a corporal who was commanding his men to lift the heavy log that was beyond their capacity and he was shouting at them giving orders to heave hoe this log. Well, since they couldn't do it, Washington quickly dismounted off his horse, went over, pitched in, helped get the log moved and into its place. Turning to the corporal, he asked, why didn't you help your men? Corporal said, sir, can't you help your men? you see that I'm a corporal, which Washington removed his coat and said, sir, I see that you're a corporal, but I wish you to see that I'm a general. Actually, his wife wrote it, but it contains a lot of what he said. At a particular point in his ministry, when he became popular in London as he preached, he drew a lot of attention. It wasn't unusual for, the editorial page to write about the preachers of the day. And today, probably more rare to have that occur. But the Ipswich Express, February 27, 1855, provided this editorial from their London correspondent, a clerical poltroon. There is some little excitement, in the religious world created by a young man, a Baptist minister, and whose father, I am told, is an independent minister of the name of Spurgeon in Colchester. This youth is fluent, and the consequences are most distressing. As his own chapel is under repair, he preaches in Exeter Hall every Sunday, and the place is crammed to suffocation. Always discontal. courses are redolent of bad taste are vulgar and theatrical, and yet he is so run after, unless you go a half an hour before the time, you will not be able to get in at all. I am told one leading minister of the independent denomination, after hearing this precocious youth, said that the exhibition was an insult to God and man. Actually, I hear the other Sunday. the gifted divine had the imprudence before preaching to say, as there were many young ladies present, that he was engaged, that his heart was, his heart it was another's. He wished them clearly to understand that he might have no presence sent to him, no attentions paid to him, no worsted slippers worked for him by the young ladies present. I suppose that the dear divine, has been rendered uncomfortable by the fondness of his female auditors. At any rate, such is the impression he wishes to leave. The only impression, however, he seems to have produced upon the judicious few is one of intense sorrow and regret that such things should be and that such a man should draw. Now, Spurgeon's feelings about this are recorded in a letter that he sent his father, a few days later of March 4, 1855, dear father, do you not be grieved at the slanderous libel in this week's express? Of course it's all lie without an atom of foundation, and while the whole of London is talking of me and thousands are unable to get near the door, the opinion of a penny-eliner is of little consequence. I beseeched you not to write but if you can see Mr. Harvey or some official it might do good. A full reply on all points will appear next week. I only fear for you. I do not like you to be grieved. For myself, I will rejoice. The devil is roused. The church is awakened, and I am now counted worthy to suffer for Christ's sake. Good ballast, Father, good ballast. Jesus' answering said unto them, they that are whole need not a physician, but they that are sick. I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance. Now, let me simply say this. There's no one who is really whole or righteous. You must realize that. All have need of the great physician, but many people do not recognize this truth. They deny that they are sick. Eventually, the dirt debris would all slowly settle down to the bottom of the water. And if you looked at the glass after a while, it would appear to be fairly clean at the top in the middle. You could see through it. This is the way. way sinners are. The simple stirring of that water, however, brings it back to its state of mud. You know, it's all dirty again if you just stir it up. The heart of man is full of the dirt, of sinful lusts, and carnal desires. However, when those evil lust and desires are not stirred up, you might imagine that your heart is pretty clean. You might love you, say, I'm a pretty good guy. you know, I don't have any major sins in my life and so forth. So you might think, I'm righteous. I'm good. I don't need a savior. So you must realize nobody is really whole or righteous. But you need to realize that you're spiritually sick. Now, sin is the sickness of the soul, not the body, but the soul. It is a deforming, weakening, wasting, and killing disease. The picture of the soul. The picture of a deforming, weakening, sin as a disease really doesn't portray to us all the heinous nature of sin. It's just a way for us to get a handle on how bad sin is. The idea of sickness doesn't capture perhaps the perverseness of the will, or the evil rebelliousness of man's nature, or the enmity and hatred of man toward God. But it's just a way to view sin from a different perspective and get a handle on it. Think about this. Sin is an hereditary disease. You're born in sin. You're shaping in iniquity. And sin did your mother conceive you? For Adam's original transgression is conferred to you. So it's hereditary. It's also a disabling disease. Those who are spiritually sick cannot please God. You can't do good. You're incapable of seeking after God, of praying and worshiping God right, of understanding the ways of God, or exhibiting any spiritual strength towards your salvation. It is a polluting disease. You defile everything you touch. If you're evil, you make others around you evil. Your children imitate your vices. You influence your family, your friends, your neighbors, your coworkers by your behavior. It's polluting. It's an incurable disease.
